{{Short description|Nuclear reaction splitting a heavy nucleus into lighter nuclei}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Nuclear fission
| image = Nuclear fission.svg
| caption = A [[uranium-235]] nucleus absorbs a [[neutron]] and splits into lighter nuclei, releasing energy and more neutrons.
| topics = [[Nuclear physics]], [[Atomic nucleus]], [[Neutron]], [[Binding energy]], [[Mass–energy equivalence]]
}}

'''Nuclear fission''' is a [[nuclear reaction]] in which the [[atomic nucleus|nucleus]] of an [[atom]] splits into two or more smaller, lighter nuclei, along with the release of [[energy]], [[gamma ray]]s, and free [[neutron]]s. The process can occur spontaneously ([[spontaneous fission]]) or be induced by bombarding a heavy nucleus with neutrons or other particles.

Fission of heavy elements is an [[exothermic reaction]] that releases millions of times more energy per reaction than [[chemical reaction]]s such as burning.

== Mechanism ==
When a heavy nucleus like [[uranium-235]] or [[plutonium-239]] absorbs a [[neutron]], it becomes an excited [[compound nucleus]]. This excitation deforms the nucleus; the [[strong interaction|nuclear force]] (short-range attractive) and [[electric field|Coulomb repulsion]] (long-range, between protons) compete. If the deformation overcomes the fission barrier, the nucleus splits.

For <sup>235</sup>U:
:<math>^{235}\text{U} + n \rightarrow ^{236}\text{U}^* \rightarrow \text{fission products} + (2-3)n + \sim200\ \text{MeV}</math>

== Energy release ==
The energy release comes from the difference in [[binding energy per nucleon]] between heavy and medium-mass nuclei (peak at [[iron-56]]). Typical fission releases about 200 MeV per event.

Distribution of energy (typical):
* Kinetic energy of fission products: ~170 MeV
* Prompt neutrons: ~5 MeV
* Prompt gamma rays: ~7 MeV
* Beta and gamma decay of fission products: ~18 MeV

== Chain reaction ==
{{Main|Nuclear chain reaction}}
Fission releases 2–3 [[neutron]]s, which can induce further fissions in a [[nuclear chain reaction]]:
* Subcritical: <math>k < 1</math> — reaction dies out
* Critical: <math>k = 1</math> — steady state ([[nuclear reactor]])
* Supercritical: <math>k > 1</math> — exponential growth ([[nuclear weapon]])

== Applications ==
* [[Nuclear power]] — electricity generation via controlled fission chain reactions
* [[Nuclear weapon]]s — uncontrolled supercritical chain reaction
* [[Nuclear marine propulsion]] — submarines, aircraft carriers
* [[Research reactor]]s — neutron sources for science
* [[Nuclear medicine]] — production of [[radioisotope]]s

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Atomic nucleus]]
* [[Binding energy]]
* [[Mass–energy equivalence]]
* [[Neutron]]
* [[Nuclear fusion]]
* [[Nuclear physics]]
* [[Nuclear reactor]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Nuclear fission]]
[[Category:Nuclear physics]]
[[Category:Nuclear technology]]
